content.text: Arize Phoenix is an open-source AI observability platform built on top of OpenTelemetry. You run it locally as a Python process or Docker container, and your agent sends traces to it over the standard OTLP HTTP protocol. Phoenix uses the OpenInference semantic convention — a layer on top of OpenTelemetry that adds LLM-specific span attributes such as llm.model_name, llm.input_messages, and llm.token_count.prompt. Because it speaks plain OTLP, any instrumented code works with Phoenix without any Phoenix-specific SDK in production.

The platform has four main areas: a trace viewer with waterfall timelines for every LLM call and tool invocation, an evaluation layer (llm_classify) that runs LLM-as-judge evals on your spans, a dataset and experiment system for regression testing prompts, and a query API (px.Client) for pulling span data into a pandas DataFrame for offline analysis.

attrs.alt: Arize Phoenix architecture: agent code with OpenInference instrumentors sends OTLP spans to the Phoenix server, which serves the UI and eval store

content.text: Install the Phoenix package along with the OpenInference instrumentor for your LLM provider. The instrumentors are thin wrappers that patch the provider SDK and emit spans automatically.
pip install arize-phoenix openinference-instrumentation-anthropic openinference-instrumentation-openai openinference-instrumentation-langchain
Start Phoenix in one line — it opens a local server at http://localhost:6006 and returns a session object you can inspect:

import phoenix as px

session = px.launch_app()   # starts Phoenix at http://localhost:6006
print(session.url)           # http://localhost:6006
For production or containerised environments, run Phoenix via Docker and point your OTLP exporter at it:

docker run -p 6006:6006 -p 4317:4317 arizephoenix/phoenix:latest
# OTLP/HTTP on :6006/v1/traces   OTLP/gRPC on :4317

Each OpenInference instrumentor patches the provider SDK at import time. You wire up a TracerProvider with an OTLP exporter, call instrumentor.instrument(), and every subsequent API call produces a span automatically — no changes to your agent code.

from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import SimpleSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from openinference.instrumentation.anthropic import AnthropicInstrumentor

PHOENIX_URL = "http://localhost:6006/v1/traces"

provider = TracerProvider()
provider.add_span_processor(
    SimpleSpanProcessor(OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=PHOENIX_URL))
)

# Patch anthropic globally — all client.messages.create() calls are now traced
AnthropicInstrumentor().instrument(tracer_provider=provider)

import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()

response = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
    max_tokens=512,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain OTLP in one sentence."}],
)
# A span named 'anthropic.messages' appears in Phoenix with:
#   llm.model_name = claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
#   llm.input_messages  (serialised message list)
#   llm.output_messages (assistant response)
#   llm.token_count.prompt / llm.token_count.completion
print(response.content[0].text)
For LangChain, swap in LangChainInstrumentor. For OpenAI, use OpenAIInstrumentor. The API is identical — just call .instrument(tracer_provider=provider) on each instrumentor you want active.

from openinference.instrumentation.openai import OpenAIInstrumentor
from openinference.instrumentation.langchain import LangChainInstrumentor

OpenAIInstrumentor().instrument(tracer_provider=provider)
LangChainInstrumentor().instrument(tracer_provider=provider)

When you write custom agent logic that is not covered by an auto-instrumentor — a planner loop, a memory lookup, a custom tool — you add spans manually using the standard OpenTelemetry tracer. Set attributes using the SpanAttributes constants from the openinference-semantic-conventions package so Phoenix renders them with the right labels.

from opentelemetry import trace as otel_trace
from openinference.semconv.trace import SpanAttributes

tracer = otel_trace.get_tracer(__name__)

def run_agent_turn(user_message: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent_turn") as span:
        span.set_attribute(
            SpanAttributes.OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND, "AGENT"
        )
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.INPUT_VALUE, user_message)

        tool_result = call_tool(user_message)
        response = call_llm_with_context(user_message, tool_result)

        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.OUTPUT_VALUE, response)
        return response

def call_tool(query: str) -> str:
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("web_search") as span:
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND, "TOOL")
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.TOOL_NAME, "web_search")
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.INPUT_VALUE, query)
        result = _do_search(query)
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.OUTPUT_VALUE, result)
        return result

The with tracer.start_as_current_span block automatically handles the parent-child relationship through OpenTelemetry's context propagation — any span created inside the block becomes a child, giving you the nested waterfall view in Phoenix without passing IDs manually.
OpenInference Span Kinds and Key Attributes
Phoenix classifies spans by their OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND attribute. Each kind unlocks a specialised view in the UI and populates its own columns in the spans DataFrame you get from px.Client.

Phoenix ships a built-in evaluation library that uses an LLM judge to classify spans. You pull the spans from the server as a DataFrame, run llm_classify with a built-in or custom template, and log the results back so they appear alongside the spans in the UI.

import phoenix as px
from phoenix.evals import (
    HallucinationEvaluator,
    QACorrectnessEvaluator,
    llm_classify,
)
from phoenix.trace import SpanEvaluations
from anthropic import Anthropic

# Pull recent spans for the 'support-agent' project
client = px.Client()
spans_df = client.get_spans_dataframe(
    project_name="support-agent",
    root_spans_only=True,
)
print(spans_df[["name", "latency_ms", "status_code"]].head())

# LLM judge — use Anthropic claude-haiku-4-5-20251001
from phoenix.evals.models import AnthropicModel
eval_model = AnthropicModel(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

halluc_evaluator = HallucinationEvaluator(eval_model)
qa_evaluator = QACorrectnessEvaluator(eval_model)

halluc_results = llm_classify(
    dataframe=spans_df,
    template=halluc_evaluator,
    model=eval_model,
    rails=["hallucinated", "factual"],
    provide_explanation=True,   # adds an 'explanation' column
)

qa_results = llm_classify(
    dataframe=spans_df,
    template=qa_evaluator,
    model=eval_model,
    rails=["correct", "incorrect"],
)

# Log eval results back to Phoenix so they appear in the UI
client.log_evaluations(
    SpanEvaluations(eval_name="hallucination", dataframe=halluc_results),
    SpanEvaluations(eval_name="qa_correctness", dataframe=qa_results),
)

The halluc_results DataFrame has one row per span: a label column ("hallucinated" or "factual"), a score column (0.0 or 1.0), and an explanation column when provide_explanation=True. After log_evaluations, each span shows the eval scores in the Phoenix sidebar.

When the built-in evaluators do not match your task, write a template string with {input} and {output} placeholders and pass a custom rail list. Phoenix fills the placeholders from each span's attributes and asks the judge model to pick one rail.

from phoenix.evals import llm_classify
from phoenix.evals.templates import PromptTemplate

TONE_TEMPLATE = PromptTemplate(
    template="""\
You are a tone classifier. Read this customer support response and decide if the tone is
professional or unprofessional. Return only one word: 'professional' or 'unprofessional'.

Response: {output}
""",
    rails=["professional", "unprofessional"],
)

tone_results = llm_classify(
    dataframe=spans_df,
    template=TONE_TEMPLATE,
    model=eval_model,
    rails=["professional", "unprofessional"],
)

print(tone_results["label"].value_counts())
# professional      47
# unprofessional     3

Phoenix's experiment system lets you snapshot a set of inputs-expected-outputs into a dataset, run your agent against every example, and compare results across runs. Each run of a dataset becomes an experiment with its own eval scores, so you can see whether a prompt change improved or regressed on the benchmark.

import pandas as pd
import phoenix as px
from phoenix.experiments import run_experiment, evaluate_experiment
from phoenix.evals import QACorrectnessEvaluator, llm_classify
from phoenix.evals.models import AnthropicModel

client = px.Client()

# Build a dataset from a CSV of question/answer pairs
df = pd.DataFrame([
    {"question": "What is RAG?", "expected": "Retrieval-Augmented Generation"},
    {"question": "What is OTLP?", "expected": "OpenTelemetry Protocol"},
])

dataset = client.upload_dataset(
    dataframe=df,
    dataset_name="qa-benchmark-v1",
    input_keys=["question"],
    output_keys=["expected"],
)

# Define a task: runs once per dataset row
def my_task(example: dict) -> dict:
    answer = call_agent(example["question"])   # your agent under test
    return {"response": answer}

# Run experiment: executes my_task for every row, traces each call
experiment = run_experiment(
    dataset=dataset,
    task=my_task,
    experiment_name="agent-v2",
)

# Evaluate the experiment output
eval_model = AnthropicModel(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
qa_eval = QACorrectnessEvaluator(eval_model)

evaluate_experiment(
    experiment,
    evaluators=[qa_eval],
)
# Results now appear in the Phoenix UI under Experiments -> agent-v2

px.Client wraps the Phoenix REST API and returns pandas DataFrames. You use it for offline analysis, building dashboards, or feeding spans into your own evaluation pipeline outside of Phoenix's built-in eval runner.

import phoenix as px
import pandas as pd
from datetime import datetime, timedelta, timezone

client = px.Client()

# All spans in the last 24 hours
spans_df = client.get_spans_dataframe(
    project_name="support-agent",
    start_time=datetime.now(timezone.utc) - timedelta(hours=24),
)

# Root spans only — one row per user request
root_df = client.get_spans_dataframe(
    project_name="support-agent",
    root_spans_only=True,
)

# Latency distribution
print(root_df["latency_ms"].describe())
# p95 latency
print(f"p95: {root_df['latency_ms'].quantile(0.95):.0f} ms")

# Error rate
error_rate = (root_df["status_code"] == "ERROR").mean()
print(f"Error rate: {error_rate:.1%}")

# LLM spans — token usage
llm_df = spans_df[spans_df["span_kind"] == "LLM"]
total_tokens = (
    llm_df["llm.token_count.prompt"].fillna(0)
    + llm_df["llm.token_count.completion"].fillna(0)
).sum()
print(f"Total tokens (last 24h): {total_tokens:,.0f}")

For multi-turn agents, you attach a session_id attribute to every root span so Phoenix can group all turns from one conversation. Set it on the span at the start of the agent entry point. Phoenix's Session view then shows the full conversation timeline side by side.

import uuid
from opentelemetry import trace as otel_trace
from openinference.semconv.trace import SpanAttributes

tracer = otel_trace.get_tracer(__name__)

def handle_user_message(session_id: str, message: str) -> str:
    """Entry point called once per turn; session_id is stable across turns."""
    with tracer.start_as_current_span("agent_turn") as span:
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.OPENINFERENCE_SPAN_KIND, "AGENT")
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.SESSION_ID, session_id)
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.INPUT_VALUE, message)
        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.USER_ID, "user-42")

        response = _run_turn(message)

        span.set_attribute(SpanAttributes.OUTPUT_VALUE, response)
        return response

# Caller:
sid = str(uuid.uuid4())                         # created once per conversation
handle_user_message(sid, "What is OpenTelemetry?")
handle_user_message(sid, "How does OTLP work?")  # same session_id
# Phoenix groups both turns under one Session entry
Comparing Phoenix with LangSmith and Langfuse
OTel-native stacks, RAG evals, no vendor lock-in

Run Phoenix evals in CI by launching Phoenix, running your test suite to produce traces, pulling the results, and failing the build if quality falls below a threshold. Because Phoenix launches in-process, no external service is needed in the pipeline.

import sys
import phoenix as px
from phoenix.evals import QACorrectnessEvaluator, llm_classify
from phoenix.trace import SpanEvaluations
from phoenix.evals.models import AnthropicModel

MIN_QA_SCORE = 0.85

px.launch_app()                         # ephemeral in-process Phoenix
run_test_suite()                         # exercise your agent, producing traces

client = px.Client()
spans_df = client.get_spans_dataframe(project_name="ci", root_spans_only=True)

eval_model = AnthropicModel(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")
results = llm_classify(
    dataframe=spans_df,
    template=QACorrectnessEvaluator(eval_model),
    model=eval_model,
    rails=["correct", "incorrect"],
)

client.log_evaluations(SpanEvaluations(eval_name="qa", dataframe=results))

mean_score = (results["label"] == "correct").mean()
print(f"QA correctness: {mean_score:.1%}")

if mean_score < MIN_QA_SCORE:
    print(f"FAIL: {mean_score:.1%} below threshold {MIN_QA_SCORE:.1%}")
    sys.exit(1)

print("PASS")

Arize Phoenix is an open-source observability platform built on OpenTelemetry and the OpenInference semantic convention. You launch it with px.launch_app() or Docker, wire up an OTLPSpanExporter, and call an OpenInference instrumentor to capture every LLM call, tool invocation, retrieval step, and embedding without touching your agent's business logic. For spans your agent controls, you add them with the standard OTel context manager and tag them with SpanAttributes constants. Phoenix's eval library (llm_classify) runs LLM-as-judge evaluations on any span DataFrame and logs scores back to the server. The experiment system lets you run your agent against a fixed dataset and compare evals across runs to detect regressions before deployment.
